question_text: Two students, Alex and Jordan, each eat an identical 2000 kJ meal. Alex then sits reading for 2 hours (using 300 kJ), while Jordan runs for 1 hour (using 2500 kJ). Which statement best explains what happens to the remaining energy from their meals?

options.label: A
options.text: Alex's remaining energy (1700 kJ) is stored as fat in his body, while Jordan's energy deficit (-500 kJ) means he loses body mass.

options.label: B
options.text: Both students store the same amount of energy as fat because they ate the same meal, regardless of their activity level.

options.label: C
options.text: Jordan uses more energy so he needs to eat more food immediately to replace the -500 kJ deficit or he will stop functioning.

options.label: D
options.text: The energy not used for activity in both cases is completely lost as heat and cannot be stored in the body.

explanation: Alex uses only 300 kJ, so 1700 kJ remains and is stored (mainly as body fat). Jordan uses 2500 kJ but only consumed 2000 kJ, creating a deficit of 500 kJ that must come from stored body energy (like fat reserves), causing a net loss of body mass. This demonstrates how energy balance determines whether we gain or lose mass.
